section.title: Jacob Blesses Ephraim and Manasseh
section.sectionRationale: The dying Jacob rallies to adopt Joseph's two Egyptian-born sons as his own and to bless them. With crossed hands he deliberately sets the younger Ephraim above the elder Manasseh, once again overturning human expectation, and he blesses in the name of the God who has shepherded and redeemed him all his life.
section.thesis: As Jacob adopts and blesses Ephraim and Manasseh, God's freedom to elevate the younger and His faithfulness as shepherd and redeemer are put on display, carrying the covenant promise into the next generation.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: Jacob adopts Joseph's sons and recalls the covenant; the crossed hands and the blessing of the God who shepherds and redeems; and the younger set before the elder with a word of God's future faithfulness.

units.label: Jacob adopts Joseph's sons
units.note: they shall be reckoned as mine

verses.text: Some time later Joseph was told, “Your father is ill.” So he set out with his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim.
When Jacob was told, “Your son Joseph has come to you,” Israel rallied his strength and sat up in bed.
Jacob said to Joseph, “{{elshaddai|God Almighty}} appeared to me at Luz in the land of Canaan, and there He blessed me
and told me, ‘Behold, I will make you fruitful and multiply you; I will make you a multitude of peoples, and will give this land to your descendants after you as an everlasting possession.’
And now your two sons born to you in Egypt before I came to you here shall be reckoned as mine; Ephraim and Manasseh shall be mine, just as Reuben and Simeon are mine.
Any children born to you after them shall be yours, and they shall be called by the names of their brothers in the territory they inherit.

Now as for me, when I was returning from Paddan, to my sorrow Rachel died along the way in the land of Canaan, some distance from Ephrath. So I buried her there beside the road to Ephrath” (that is, Bethlehem).

units.label: The crossed hands
units.note: the God who has been my shepherd

verses.text: When Israel saw the sons of Joseph, he asked, “Who are these?”
Joseph said to his father, “They are the sons God has given me in this place.” So Jacob said, “Please bring them to me, that I may {{barak|bless}} them.”
Now Israel’s eyesight was poor because of old age; he could hardly see. Joseph brought his sons to him, and his father kissed them and embraced them.
“I never expected to see your face again,” Israel said to Joseph, “but now God has let me see your children as well.”
Then Joseph removed his sons from his father’s knees and bowed facedown.
And Joseph took both of them, with Ephraim in his right hand toward Israel’s left hand, and Manasseh in his left hand toward Israel’s right hand, and brought them close to him.
But Israel stretched out his right hand and put it on the head of Ephraim, the younger; and crossing his hands, he put his left on Manasseh’s head, although Manasseh was the {{bekor|firstborn}}.
Then he blessed Joseph and said: “May the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked, the God who has been my shepherd all my life to this day,

the angel who has {{gaal|redeemed}} me from all harm, may He bless these boys. And may they be called by my name and the names of my fathers Abraham and Isaac, and may they grow into a multitude upon the earth.”
When Joseph saw that his father had placed his right hand on Ephraim’s head, he was displeased and took his father’s hand to move it from Ephraim’s head to Manasseh’s.
“Not so, my father!” Joseph said. “This one is the firstborn; put your right hand on his head.”

But his father refused. “I know, my son, I know!” he said. “He too shall become a people, and he too shall be great; nevertheless, his younger brother shall be greater than he, and his offspring shall become a multitude of nations.”
So that day Jacob blessed them and said: “By you shall Israel pronounce this blessing: ‘May God make you like Ephraim and Manasseh.’” So he put Ephraim before Manasseh.
Then Israel said to Joseph, “Look, I am about to die, but God will be with you and bring you back to the land of your fathers.
And to you, as one who is above your brothers, I give the ridge of land that I took from the Amorites with my sword and bow.”

elshaddai.gloss: God Almighty, the name by which God appeared to the patriarchs with promises of fruitfulness and land. Jacob recalls this appearance at Luz as the ground of the blessing he now passes on. The name anchors the whole scene in the covenant God made with Abraham and Isaac.

barak.gloss: To bless, to speak favor and God's good gifts over another. Jacob's dying act is to bless, transmitting the covenant blessing to a new generation. In Scripture such blessing is not mere well-wishing but a powerful word tied to God's purpose.

gaal.gloss: To redeem, to act as a kinsman who rescues and buys back. Jacob calls God the angel who has redeemed him from all harm. The word looks ahead to God as the great Redeemer of His people, ultimately in Christ.

bekor.gloss: The firstborn, who by custom held the chief inheritance and rank. Jacob crosses his hands to put the younger before the firstborn, as God had done with Jacob himself. The word highlights how God's choice repeatedly overturns human precedence.

basic.context: The dying Jacob adopts Joseph's two Egyptian-born sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, as his own, giving Joseph a double portion. Then, with crossed hands, he blesses the younger above the elder. The key question is why Jacob deliberately reverses the expected order of the firstborn.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative that turns on a deliberate, Spirit-guided act: the crossing of the hands. The main question is whether Jacob has simply made a mistake because of his blindness. The text is clear that he knows exactly what he is doing.
basic.originalLanguages: Jacob invokes El Shaddai, God Almighty, the covenant name, and blesses, barak, the boys. He names God as the one who has been his shepherd and the angel who has redeemed, gaal, him. Though Manasseh is the bekor, the firstborn, God's choice again elevates the younger.
basic.typology: The pattern of the younger chosen over the elder runs through Genesis and points beyond merit to grace. Jacob names God as his shepherd and redeemer, titles fulfilled in Christ the good Shepherd and the true Redeemer of His people.
basic.christ: Jacob blesses in the name of the God who shepherded and redeemed him, longing that his sons be gathered under the same care. Both titles find their fullness in Jesus, the Shepherd who lays down His life and the Redeemer who buys His people back from all harm.
basic.god: God is praised as the covenant-keeping Almighty, the lifelong shepherd, and the redeemer from all harm. He is free to bless as He chooses, lifting the younger above the elder by grace. His faithfulness spans the generations from Abraham to these boys.
basic.crossReferences: The younger-over-elder pattern echoes Isaac over Ishmael and Jacob over Esau. Ephraim's elevation shapes later Israelite history, and the shepherd and redeemer titles flower in Psalm 23 and in Christ.
basic.oneStory: The covenant blessing is handed down to a new generation, and the tribes of Israel take shape. God's gracious freedom and covenant faithfulness carry the promise forward toward the nation and the Messiah.

context.literary: A deathbed blessing scene, echoing Isaac's blessing of Jacob but without deception; here the aged father, though dim of sight, blesses knowingly. The crossing of hands is the dramatic center, and the poem of blessing gathers Jacob's whole life under God as shepherd and redeemer.
context.historical: Adoption formulas and the double portion of the firstborn were known in the ancient Near East; by claiming Joseph's two sons, Jacob grants Joseph a double inheritance through them. Ephraim and Manasseh will become full tribes, so the reversal here carries lasting weight.
context.canonical: Ephraim's precedence anticipates the tribe's later prominence in the northern kingdom. The scene continues Genesis's recurring theme that God's election does not follow human primogeniture, a theme Paul later presses in Romans to explain grace.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the transmission of the covenant blessing to Joseph's sons, to affirm God's free choice of the younger, and to praise God as shepherd and redeemer. The narrator wants Israel to see how the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh came to be reckoned among the sons of Israel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Jacob's adoption and blessing; it does not prescribe crossing hands as a ritual. The abiding truths are God's sovereign, gracious election, His covenant faithfulness across generations, and His character as shepherd and redeemer.

debates.question: Is Jacob's placing of the younger above the elder a mistake or a deliberate act?

views.label: Deliberate and Spirit-guided
views.case: The text stresses that Jacob crossed his hands intentionally and, when corrected, said I know, my son, I know; his blindness does not cause the reversal.

views.label: Joseph's misreading
views.case: Joseph assumes it is an error of failing eyesight and tries to correct it, which highlights, by contrast, that Jacob acts with full intent and prophetic insight.

debates.bearing: The scene teaches that God's blessing follows His gracious choice, not human rules of precedence, a pattern seen throughout Genesis and fulfilled in the gospel of grace.

study.originalLanguages: Jacob grounds the blessing in El Shaddai, God Almighty, the covenant name from his encounter at Luz. He blesses, barak, the boys not with mere good wishes but with a weighty word tied to God's purpose, calling God his lifelong shepherd and the angel who has gaal, redeemed, him from all harm, the language of a kinsman who rescues and buys back. Though Manasseh is the bekor, the firstborn, Jacob crosses his hands so the right hand rests on Ephraim, enacting once more God's freedom to raise the younger by grace rather than birth order.

typology.type: The younger chosen over the firstborn
typology.fulfillment: The gospel pattern of grace over natural precedence, gathering those the world overlooks into Christ.

typology.type: God my shepherd all my life
typology.fulfillment: Jesus the good Shepherd who leads and keeps His flock to the end.

typology.type: The angel who redeemed me from all harm
typology.fulfillment: Christ the Redeemer who buys His people back from sin and death.

study.christ: Jacob blesses in the name of the God who walked with his fathers, shepherded him all his life, and redeemed him from all harm, and he longs for his grandsons to be kept by that same God. Both titles are fulfilled in Jesus: the good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, and the Redeemer, the true kinsman, who buys His people back from every harm. And the crossing of the hands, lifting the younger over the elder, embodies the grace that runs to its climax in Christ, who welcomes the last and the least and makes them heirs.
study.god: God is celebrated in this chapter as the covenant Almighty who keeps His promise across generations, the shepherd who has fed and guided Jacob every day of his life, and the redeemer who has rescued him from all harm. He is sovereignly free, blessing the younger above the elder not by human merit or custom but by His own gracious choice. The same God who elevated Jacob himself now elevates Ephraim, showing that the covenant runs on grace.

crossReferences.note: The older shall serve the younger, the pattern of God's choice seen with Jacob and Esau.
Joseph's fuller blessing, continuing the double portion given through his sons.
The LORD is my shepherd, echoing Jacob's confession of God's lifelong care.
Paul uses God's free choice of the younger to explain election by grace.

study.oneStory: The covenant blessing passes to a new generation as Jacob adopts and blesses Joseph's sons, and the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh are grafted into Israel. God's gracious freedom to lift the younger and His faithfulness as shepherd and redeemer carry the promise forward. The line advances toward the nation and, beyond it, to the Shepherd and Redeemer, Jesus, in whom the blessing of Abraham reaches the world.

quiz.q: What does Jacob do with Joseph's two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh?

options.text: He adopts them as his own, granting Joseph a double portion, and blesses them.
options.note: Reckoning them as his own gives Joseph a double inheritance through them.

options.text: He sends them away because they were born in Egypt.
options.note: The opposite; he embraces them and claims them as his own sons.

options.text: He refuses to acknowledge them at all.
options.note: He gladly receives them, saying God has let him see Joseph's children.

options.text: He gives them to Pharaoh to raise.
options.note: He keeps them within the covenant family and blesses them.

quiz.q: What is significant about Jacob crossing his hands during the blessing?

options.text: He deliberately sets the younger Ephraim above the elder Manasseh.
options.note: God's choice again overturns the expected order of the firstborn.

options.text: He simply could not see which boy was which.
options.note: Joseph thinks so, but Jacob says I know, my son, I know; the act is intentional.

options.text: He was blessing only one of the two boys.
options.note: He blesses both, but places the younger first.

options.text: He accidentally blessed Joseph instead of the sons.
options.note: He knowingly blesses the boys, hands crossed on purpose.

quiz.q: How does Jacob describe God in his blessing?

options.text: As his lifelong shepherd and the angel who has redeemed him from all harm.
options.note: These titles point ahead to Christ the Shepherd and Redeemer.

options.text: As a distant God who left him to fend for himself.
options.note: The opposite; Jacob confesses God's constant care and rescue.

options.text: As a God he has only just come to know.
options.note: He speaks of God's care all my life to this day.

options.text: As a God who blesses only the strong and firstborn.
options.note: The blessing highlights grace to the younger, not the strong.

quiz.q: How does Joseph react to the crossed hands, and how does Jacob answer?

options.text: Joseph tries to correct it; Jacob refuses, saying the younger will be greater.
options.note: Jacob knowingly upholds God's choice of Ephraim.

options.text: Joseph approves at once and says nothing.
options.note: Joseph is actually displeased and tries to move his father's hand.

options.text: Jacob apologizes and switches his hands.
options.note: Jacob refuses to switch; he acts with full intent.

options.text: Joseph removes his sons in anger.
options.note: Joseph objects but does not withdraw them; the blessing stands.

quiz.q: What larger biblical pattern does this scene illustrate?

options.text: God's gracious freedom to choose and bless apart from human precedence.
options.note: As with Jacob over Esau, God elevates the younger by grace.

options.text: That the firstborn always receives the greater blessing.
options.note: The scene overturns exactly that expectation.

options.text: That blessings depend on a father's eyesight.
options.note: Jacob's blindness is noted, but the choice is deliberate, not accidental.

options.text: That God's promises had come to an end.
options.note: The blessing carries the covenant promise forward to a new generation.

questions: Jacob looked back over his whole life and called God his shepherd. As you look back over yours, where can you trace God's shepherding care?
God repeatedly chose the younger, the overlooked, the unexpected. How does it encourage you that God's blessing runs on grace rather than rank or merit?
Jacob called God the one who redeemed him from all harm. What harm has God brought you through that you can name and give thanks for?
Near death, Jacob's great concern was to pass on blessing to the next generation. Whom are you called to bless and speak God's good over?
Joseph wanted the blessing to follow his own plan, but God's choice differed. Where might you need to release your expectations and trust God's way?

translationNotes.note: Paddan is short for Paddan-aram, that is, northwest Mesopotamia; Jacob recalls Rachel's death near Ephrath, which the text identifies as Bethlehem.
The angel who has redeemed me uses the kinsman-redeemer language, gaal; the same word underlies the great biblical theme of God as Redeemer of His people.
The word rendered ridge of land is shekem, which sounds like the town and district of Shechem; the phrase can also be read as one portion above your brothers.
